title: What is YOLO?
concept: YOLO redefines object detection as a single regression problem, allowing it to predict bounding boxes and classes in one forward pass.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between image classification and object detection?

options.text: Classification labels the whole image, while detection locates objects with bounding boxes
Classification only works on black and white images
Detection is much faster and does not use neural networks

exercises.explain: Correct! Classification identifies what is in the image, whereas detection also finds where it is using boxes.

cards.title: A Catch in a Glance
cards.body: Imagine a baseball catcher catching a fast pitch. They don't look at the ball, classify it, and then calculate its location separately. They do it all in a single glance! YOLO does the exact same thing for computer vision.

cards.title: What is YOLO?
cards.body: YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a single-stage object detection algorithm. Instead of looking at an image multiple times or proposing regions first, it passes the entire image through the network just once to predict bounding boxes and classes simultaneously.

cards.title: Room-by-Room vs. Aerial Photo
cards.body: Traditional detectors are like a house inspector walking room-by-room to find objects. YOLO is like taking a single high-resolution aerial photo of the entire house and pointing out all objects at once.

cards.title: Real-Time Traffic Monitoring
cards.body: A traffic camera using YOLO looks at a video frame and instantly draws boxes around cars, pedestrians, and traffic lights, calculating their classes and locations at 60 frames per second.

cards.title: Speed vs. Accuracy
cards.body: You might think YOLO must be highly inaccurate because it is so fast. In reality, modern YOLO versions are both incredibly fast and competitive with the most accurate slower detectors.

cards.title: Single-Stage vs. Two-Stage
cards.body: How object detection approaches differ in workflow:

left.text:
1 pass: predicts boxes & classes directly
Extremely fast (real-time)
Simpler architecture

right.text:
2 passes: proposes regions, then classifies
Slower execution
More complex, historically slightly more precise

exercises.prompt: Why is YOLO called 'You Only Look Once'?

options.text: It processes the entire image in a single forward pass of the neural network
It deletes the image from memory immediately after looking at it
It can only detect one single object per image
It does not support training with backpropagation

exercises.explain: Correct! YOLO passes the image through the network once, predicting all boxes and classes simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: True or False: YOLO is a two-stage object detector that requires a separate region proposal network.
exercises.explain: False. YOLO is a single-stage detector; it predicts bounding boxes directly from the input image without a separate proposal step.

exercises.sentence: Because YOLO processes the entire image in one pass, it operates as a ___ stage detector.
exercises.explain: YOLO is the classic example of a single-stage detector.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a key advantage of YOLO's single-stage design?

options.text: Extremely fast speed suitable for real-time video
It does not require any training data
It is guaranteed to be 100% accurate

exercises.explain: Correct! YOLO's speed makes it perfect for real-time applications like self-driving cars.

exercises.prompt: A self-driving car needs to detect pedestrians instantly to avoid accidents. Why would you choose YOLO over a two-stage detector like Faster R-CNN?

options.text: YOLO's single-pass design allows it to run at high frame rates (real-time), which is critical for split-second safety decisions.
Faster R-CNN cannot detect pedestrians at all.
YOLO uses less training data than Faster R-CNN.
YOLO does not use convolutional layers, making it safer.

exercises.explain: Exactly! YOLO's speed is crucial for real-time safety systems like autonomous vehicles.
